column2: Training a model on labeled input-output pairs so it learns to predict labels for new data; used for classification and regression.
Learning structure/patterns from unlabeled data, e.g. clustering or dimensionality reduction, with no predefined target labels.
An agent learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties to maximize cumulative reward over time.
ML using multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations from large data; underpins modern vision and language models.
A neural-network architecture using self-attention to process sequences in parallel; the foundation of modern LLMs.
A large model pretrained on broad data that can be adapted (fine-tuned or prompted) to many downstream tasks.
Numerical vector representations of data that capture semantic meaning; similar items sit close together in vector space.
The unit of text (subword/word piece) an LLM processes; input/output length and cost are measured in tokens.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation augments an LLM prompt with relevant documents retrieved from an external store to ground responses.
An LLM-driven system that plans and takes actions using tools/APIs to achieve goals, often autonomously across multiple steps.
An open protocol standardizing how LLM applications connect to external tools, data sources, and context servers.
Fine-tuning updates model weights on task data; prompting steers a frozen model via instructions/examples at inference with no weight change.
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback aligns a model to human preferences using a reward model trained on human-ranked outputs.

column1: What does temperature control in an LLM?
column2: Sampling randomness: low temperature yields focused/deterministic output, high temperature yields more diverse/creative output.
When an LLM produces fluent but false or fabricated content presented as fact; maps to OWASP LLM09 Misinformation.
The maximum tokens (prompt plus output) a model can consider at once; exceeding it truncates or drops earlier content.
Training builds/updates model weights from data; inference uses the trained model to generate outputs for new inputs.
A versioned catalog for storing, tracking, and governing models, their metadata, lineage, and lifecycle stage.
A central repository for curated, reusable ML features that ensures consistency between training and serving.
A database optimized to store embeddings and perform similarity (nearest-neighbor) search; the retrieval backbone of RAG.
A proxy/control point in front of model endpoints providing auth, rate limiting, routing, guardrails, logging, and policy enforcement.
Practices and tooling to build, deploy, monitor, and maintain ML/LLM systems reliably across their lifecycle.

column1: How does AI expand the attack surface?
column2: It adds data, models, prompts, agents/tools, and the supply chain as new attack vectors, plus non-determinism and data-centric risk.

column1: Why does AI non-determinism matter for security?
column2: Identical inputs can yield different outputs, complicating testing, detection, reproducibility, and deterministic controls.

column1: What is dual-use risk in AI?
column2: The same AI capability can serve beneficial or malicious purposes, e.g. code generation aiding developers or malware authors.

column1: What is the CIA triad applied to AI?
column2: Confidentiality (model/data/prompt secrecy), Integrity (no poisoning/tampering), Availability (resist DoS); AI adds safety, privacy, fairness.
Building security and safety into AI from inception (threat modeling, guardrails, least privilege) rather than bolting it on later.

column1: What are the four NIST AI RMF functions?
column2: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage.
A knowledge base of adversarial tactics and techniques against AI/ML systems, modeled after MITRE ATT&CK.
An international standard specifying requirements for an AI Management System (AIMS) for responsible governance of AI.
Secure AI Framework: Google's conceptual framework of best practices for securing AI systems.

column1: What is the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications?
column2: A community-curated list of the most critical security risks in LLM apps; the 2025 edition spans LLM01 through LLM10.

column1: What is the OWASP ML Security Top 10?
column2: A list of top risks specific to traditional ML systems (e.g. data poisoning, evasion, model theft), distinct from the LLM Top 10.

column1: What is the NIST GenAI Profile (AI 600-1)?
column2: A companion to the AI RMF addressing risks unique to generative AI and suggested actions to manage them.

column1: What is the shared responsibility model for AI?
column2: Security duties split among model provider, platform/deployer, application owner, and end user; the split shifts for SaaS vs self-hosted.
With SaaS the provider secures the model/infra while you secure data, prompts, and use; self-hosting puts model, infra, and patching on you.
Crafting adversarial inputs at inference to cause misclassification while appearing normal to humans, e.g. perturbed images.
An input with small, often imperceptible perturbations engineered to fool a model into a wrong output.
Evasion attacks the model at inference with crafted inputs; poisoning attacks at training time by corrupting the data.
Injecting malicious/manipulated samples into training data to degrade performance or implant attacker-chosen behavior; OWASP LLM04.
A model that behaves normally except when a hidden trigger is present, then produces attacker-chosen output.
Reconstructing sensitive training-data features (e.g. a face) by querying a model and analyzing its outputs.
Repeatedly querying a model to replicate its functionality or parameters, stealing IP and enabling offline attacks.
Determining whether a specific record was in the training set, a privacy breach revealing data inclusion.
Inversion reconstructs training-data features/attributes; membership inference only determines if a given record was used in training.

column1: What is transferability in adversarial ML?
column2: Adversarial examples crafted against one model often fool other models, enabling black-box attacks without target access.
Inputs crafted to maximize a model's compute/energy/latency, a resource-exhaustion attack related to unbounded consumption.
Untrusted input that overrides or manipulates an LLM's instructions to produce attacker-desired behavior; OWASP LLM01.
Direct: the attacker types malicious instructions into the prompt. Indirect: malicious instructions hide in external content the LLM later ingests.
Malicious instructions embedded in third-party content (web pages, files, emails) that the LLM retrieves and follows, often unknown to the user.
A prompt-injection technique that bypasses an LLM's safety guardrails to elicit restricted or harmful output.
Exposure of the hidden system prompt, revealing secrets, logic, or guardrails an attacker can exploit; OWASP LLM07.
Downstream systems trusting raw LLM output without validation, enabling XSS, SQLi, SSRF, or RCE; OWASP LLM05.

column1: What is sensitive information disclosure in LLMs?
column2: An LLM revealing PII, secrets, or proprietary data through its outputs; OWASP LLM02.
Prompting a model to regurgitate memorized training data, potentially exposing PII or copyrighted/secret content.
Granting an LLM/agent too much functionality, permissions, or autonomy, allowing harmful actions from manipulation; OWASP LLM06.

column1: What is overreliance on LLM output?
column2: Users or systems trusting AI output without verification, propagating errors, hallucinations, or insecure code; relates to OWASP LLM09.
Allowing uncontrolled or excessive inference (queries, tokens, compute) causing DoS, cost harvesting, or model theft; OWASP LLM10.

column1: What is model DoS / cost harvesting?
column2: Driving large volumes of expensive inference to exhaust resources or inflate costs (denial of wallet); part of unbounded consumption.
Compromise via poisoned pretrained models, malicious datasets, vulnerable dependencies, or tampered model hubs; OWASP LLM03.

column1: What is unsafe deserialization in ML?
column2: Loading models in formats like Python pickle can execute arbitrary code, enabling RCE from a malicious model file.

column1: Why is the pickle format risky for models?
column2: Pickle can execute arbitrary Python on load, so a malicious model file runs code on import; prefer safetensors.
Publishing malicious models or packages with names resembling popular ones to trick users into downloading them.
Attackers tamper with or upload malicious models to a repository so downstream users pull a backdoored model.

column1: What is a poisoned pretrained model?
column2: A publicly shared model embedded with backdoors or bias that activate under attacker-chosen conditions after adoption.

column1: What is the confused-deputy problem in agents?
column2: An agent with legitimate privileges is tricked by an attacker into misusing its authority on the attacker's behalf.

column1: What is tool misuse in agentic AI?
column2: Manipulating an agent (often via injection) to invoke its tools/APIs in harmful ways beyond intended use.

column1: What is SSRF via AI agents?
column2: Tricking an agent into making server-side requests to internal/cloud resources (e.g. metadata endpoints), exposing data or enabling pivoting.
Injecting malicious content into an agent's persistent memory or conversation history so it influences later actions.
Interacting agents can amplify errors or be manipulated to coordinate harmful actions or bypass individual controls.
A compromised or malicious MCP/tool server feeds harmful data or actions to an agent, or exfiltrates data via tool calls.

column1: Why is lateral movement a risk with agents?
column2: Agents with network/tool access and credentials can be steered to reach and act on other internal systems, like an attacker foothold.
A model storing and later reproducing verbatim training data, risking PII, secret, or copyright exposure.

column1: What is RAG data leakage / broken document authorization?
column2: RAG retrieving documents a user should not access because retrieval ignores per-document permissions, leaking restricted content.
Reconstructing original text/data from its stored embedding vectors, a confidentiality risk for vector stores; OWASP LLM08.

column1: What are vector and embedding weaknesses?
column2: Risks in how embeddings/vector stores are generated, stored, and retrieved: inversion, poisoning, cross-tenant leakage, weak access control; OWASP LLM08.

column1: What is data exfiltration via LLM outputs?
column2: An attacker uses crafted prompts or injection to make the model encode and emit sensitive data through its responses.
Combining model outputs or anonymized data with other information to re-identify specific individuals.

column1: List the OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) categories.
column2: LLM01 Prompt Injection; LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure; LLM03 Supply Chain; LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning; LLM05 Improper Output Handling; LLM06 Excessive Agency; LLM07 System Prompt Leakage; LLM08 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses; LLM09 Misinformation; LLM10 Unbounded Consumption.
Prompt Injection: manipulating LLM behavior via crafted inputs, directly or indirectly.
Sensitive Information Disclosure: exposure of PII, secrets, or proprietary data through model outputs.
Supply Chain: vulnerabilities from third-party models, datasets, plugins, and dependencies.
Data and Model Poisoning: tampering with training, fine-tuning, or embedding data to introduce backdoors or bias.
Improper Output Handling: insufficient validation of LLM output before passing it to downstream systems.
Excessive Agency: harm from an LLM having too much functionality, permissions, or autonomy.
System Prompt Leakage: disclosure of system-prompt contents that exposes secrets or guardrail logic.
Vector and Embedding Weaknesses: security flaws in the embeddings and vector stores used by RAG.
Misinformation: a model producing false or misleading information (including hallucinations) that users may overrely on.
Unbounded Consumption: uncontrolled resource use enabling DoS, cost harvesting, or model extraction.

column1: ML attack vs LLM attack vs infrastructure attack?
column2: ML attacks target model math (evasion/poisoning); LLM attacks target prompts/outputs/agency; infrastructure attacks target serving hosts, registries, and dependencies.

column1: Which OWASP risk covers indirect prompt injection?
column2: LLM01 Prompt Injection (the indirect variant via external content).

column1: Which OWASP risk fits a pickle-based RCE in a downloaded model?
column2: LLM03 Supply Chain (a malicious model artifact via unsafe deserialization).

column1: What is data provenance and lineage?
column2: Tracking the origin, transformations, and movement of data through the ML pipeline to verify trust and integrity.
Vet and curate data sources, validate/sanitize inputs, anomaly/outlier detection, provenance tracking, and dataset access control.
Adding calibrated noise so outputs do not reveal whether any individual's data was included, bounding privacy loss (epsilon).

column1: What is PII minimization for AI?
column2: Collecting and retaining only necessary personal data and removing/redacting PII before training or logging.
Classification drives handling, access controls, and decisions on what may be used for training or sent to external models.
Restricting who can read or modify training datasets to preserve integrity and prevent poisoning or leakage.
Cryptographically signing model artifacts so consumers can verify integrity and authenticity before loading.
A Machine Learning Bill of Materials listing model components, datasets, and dependencies for transparency and supply-chain security.

column1: What are safetensors and why use them?
column2: A safe model serialization format storing only tensors with no executable code, preventing deserialization RCE unlike pickle.
To detect known vulnerabilities, malware, unsafe serialization, and backdoors in models and their software stack before use.

column1: What is reproducibility in secure MLOps?
column2: The ability to recreate a model from pinned data, code, and config; supports integrity, auditing, and incident investigation.

column1: What is secrets management in MLOps?
column2: Securely storing and injecting API keys, tokens, and credentials via vaults or short-lived tokens rather than hardcoding them.
Filters/validators on prompts that block injection, malicious payloads, PII, or policy-violating content before reaching the model.
Controls that validate, filter, redact, or encode model output before it reaches users or downstream systems.

column1: What does an AI gateway enforce?
column2: Centralized authentication, rate limiting, routing, guardrails, logging, and policy across model endpoints.
Isolating model-serving and training environments limits lateral movement and contains compromise.

column1: What is sandboxing for AI tool execution?
column2: Running agent-invoked code/tools in isolated, least-privilege environments so malicious actions cannot reach the host or network.

column1: What is rate limiting for AI endpoints?
column2: Capping request/token volume per user, key, or time window to prevent abuse, DoS, and cost harvesting.
Classifiers that detect and block toxic, harmful, or policy-violating input and output in AI systems.
Giving each agent its own scoped identity/credentials so its actions are authenticated, authorized, and auditable.
An agent acts using the end user's delegated permissions so it cannot exceed what that user is allowed to do.
Restricting an agent to a vetted set of approved tools/actions and denying everything else by default.
RBAC grants access by role; ABAC grants by attributes (user, resource, context), enabling finer-grained, dynamic policies.

column1: What is least privilege for AI tools?
column2: Granting agents/tools only the minimum permissions and scope needed, reducing the blast radius of compromise or manipulation.

column1: How to secure a vector database?
column2: Access control and tenant isolation, encryption, source vetting, and authorization-aware retrieval to prevent leakage and poisoning.

column1: What is document-level authorization in RAG?
column2: Enforcing per-document/user permissions at retrieval time so users only receive chunks they are authorized to see.

column1: Defenses against indirect prompt injection in RAG?
column2: Treat retrieved content as untrusted, sanitize/strip instructions, separate data from instructions, vet sources, and apply output guardrails.

column1: What is index poisoning in RAG?
column2: Inserting malicious documents into the knowledge base so retrieval surfaces attacker content; defend with source vetting and integrity checks.

column1: Why treat LLM output as untrusted?
column2: The model can be manipulated or hallucinate, so output may carry injection or malicious payloads; validate and encode before use.

column1: What is output encoding for LLM apps?
column2: Context-aware escaping of model output (HTML, SQL, shell) so it cannot execute as code in downstream systems.

column1: What are canary tokens for LLMs?
column2: Unique secret strings planted in prompts/data; their appearance in outputs or logs signals leakage or injection.
Structuring prompts to clearly separate trusted instructions from user data and constrain model behavior to reduce injection.

column1: What is input sanitization for prompts?
column2: Cleaning, normalizing, and filtering user input to remove injection patterns and malicious payloads before the model processes it.
Requiring human review and approval before an AI executes high-impact or irreversible actions.

column1: What is PII redaction in pipelines?
column2: Detecting and removing or masking personal data from prompts, logs, and training data to limit exposure.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection?
column2: Input/output guardrails, instruction-data separation, least privilege, HITL approvals, and treating all external content as untrusted.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure?
column2: Data minimization, PII redaction/scrubbing, output filtering, access controls, and limiting sensitive data in training and prompts.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain?
column2: Vet sources, verify signatures, scan models, use safetensors, maintain an MLBOM, and pin dependencies.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning?
column2: Data provenance, validation/sanitization, anomaly detection, access control, and integrity verification of datasets and models.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling?
column2: Treat output as untrusted: validate, sanitize, context-aware encode, and parameterize before use in downstream systems.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM06 Excessive Agency?
column2: Least-privilege tools/permissions, limit functionality, require human approval for high-impact actions, and scope agent access.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM07 System Prompt Leakage?
column2: Do not put secrets in the system prompt; enforce controls outside the model; assume the prompt can leak.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM08 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses?
column2: Access control and tenant isolation on vector stores, authorization-aware retrieval, encryption, and data validation.
Grounding via RAG, citations, output verification, human oversight, and user disclaimers to avoid overreliance.

column1: Defense for OWASP LLM10 Unbounded Consumption?
column2: Rate limiting, token quotas and budgets, input size caps, timeouts, monitoring, and per-user/key throttling.
Structured adversarial testing of AI to find safety and security failures (jailbreaks, injection, harmful output) before attackers do.
Automated, repeatable tests/benchmarks measuring model quality, safety, and security across defined scenarios.
To ensure model or prompt updates do not reintroduce vulnerabilities or degrade safety guardrails.

column1: What is continuous evaluation in CI/CD for AI?
column2: Running security and safety evals automatically on each model or prompt change to catch regressions before deployment.

column1: What is model validation before deployment?
column2: Verifying a model's performance, safety, robustness, and integrity meet thresholds prior to release.

column1: What does NIST AI RMF Govern do?
column2: Establishes organizational culture, policies, accountability, and processes for managing AI risk across the lifecycle.

column1: What does NIST AI RMF Map do?
column2: Establishes context and identifies and frames the risks of the AI system and its use.

column1: What does NIST AI RMF Measure do?
column2: Analyzes, assesses, benchmarks, and monitors AI risks using quantitative and qualitative methods.

column1: What does NIST AI RMF Manage do?
column2: Prioritizes and acts on risks: allocating resources, treating, and responding over time.
AI RMF is a voluntary US risk-management framework (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage); ISO 42001 is a certifiable management-system standard.
A standard for an AI Management System (AIMS): governance requirements to develop and use AI responsibly, certifiable like ISO 27001.

column1: What are the EU AI Act risk tiers?
column2: Prohibited (unacceptable), High, Limited (transparency obligations), and Minimal risk.

column1: What are prohibited AI practices under the EU AI Act?
column2: Unacceptable-risk uses banned outright, e.g. social scoring and certain manipulative or untargeted biometric practices.

column1: What are high-risk systems under the EU AI Act?
column2: Systems in sensitive domains (e.g. critical infrastructure, employment, biometrics) subject to strict requirements like risk management, data governance, and oversight.

column1: What are limited-risk obligations under the EU AI Act?
column2: Transparency duties, e.g. telling users they are interacting with AI or labeling AI-generated content.

column1: What is record-keeping under the EU AI Act?
column2: High-risk systems must maintain logs and technical documentation for traceability and compliance audits.
A requirement to inform users when they interact with AI or consume AI-generated content (e.g. EU AI Act limited-risk).
GDPR Article 22 restricts solely automated decisions with legal or significant effects and grants rights to information, human review, and contesting.
Documentation of a model's purpose, training data, performance, limitations, and ethical considerations for transparency.
A model card documents a model's behavior and limits; a datasheet documents a dataset's composition, collection, and intended use.
Documentation describing a broader AI system's components, safety evaluations, and risk mitigations beyond a single model.

column1: What is an AI impact assessment?
column2: A structured evaluation of an AI system's potential effects on rights, safety, fairness, and society before or during deployment.

column1: What is an AI risk register?
column2: A living record of identified AI risks with likelihood, impact, owners, and treatment status.

column1: What is risk acceptance for AI?
column2: A documented decision by an accountable owner to tolerate a residual risk within defined thresholds.
Defining thresholds for which AI risks are tolerable given benefits, context, and legal requirements.
Risk from external models, datasets, and AI vendors (provenance, licensing, security, reliability) you do not directly control.
A verifiable record of a model's origin, training data, and modifications supporting trust and licensing compliance.
To ensure usage rights, commercial-use terms, and data licensing comply with how you deploy the model.

column1: What is data licensing risk for AI?
column2: Using training or RAG data without proper rights can create IP, privacy, and compliance liabilities.

column1: Open vs closed model risk tradeoffs?
column2: Open/self-hosted models give control and inspectability but shift security/patching to you; closed/API models offload infra but reduce transparency and add vendor dependence.

column1: What is third-party AI vendor assessment?
column2: Evaluating an AI vendor's security, privacy, compliance, data handling, and model practices before procurement.

column1: What are bias sources in AI?
column2: Biased training data, sampling, labeling, feature choice, proxy variables, and human or historical bias baked into data.
Quantitative measures of bias across groups, e.g. demographic parity, equalized odds, and equal opportunity.
Techniques making model decisions understandable to humans (e.g. feature attribution like SHAP or LIME) for trust and accountability.
The ability for affected individuals to challenge or appeal an AI-driven decision.
Bias and variance are sources of model error; fairness concerns equitable outcomes across protected groups, a related but distinct issue.
Organizational structures (committees, policies, roles) ensuring AI is developed and used ethically, safely, and lawfully.

column1: What are the OECD AI Principles?
column2: International principles for trustworthy AI: inclusive growth, human-centered values, transparency, robustness/safety, and accountability.
Unsanctioned use of AI tools/services by employees outside governance, risking data leakage and compliance gaps.

column1: What is an enterprise AI acceptable use policy?
column2: Rules defining approved AI tools, allowed data, prohibited uses, and approval workflows for employees.

column1: What is DLP for generative AI?
column2: Controls that detect and block sensitive data from being pasted or sent into external AI tools.

column1: Which framework certifies an AI management system?
column2: ISO/IEC 42001.

column1: Which framework maps adversarial AI attack techniques?
column2: MITRE ATLAS.

column1: Which framework lists top LLM application risks?
column2: The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025).

column1: Which framework provides Govern, Map, Measure, Manage?
column2: The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF).

column1: What should AI prompt/response logging capture?
column2: Prompts, responses, tool calls, user/session identity, timestamps, and decisions, privacy-aware and tamper-resistant for audit and detection.

column1: Why log tool calls in agentic systems?
column2: To audit what actions an agent took, detect misuse or confused-deputy abuse, and support incident investigation.

column1: What is privacy-aware logging for AI?
column2: Redacting or minimizing PII and secrets in logs while retaining enough signal for security monitoring.

column1: What is audit trail integrity for AI?
column2: Tamper-evident, retained logs of AI decisions and actions to support investigations and compliance.
Degradation of model performance over time as real-world data diverges from training data; monitored for reliability and security.
Data drift is a change in the input distribution; concept drift is a change in the input-output relationship the model learned.

column1: What is drift monitoring's security value?
column2: Sudden drift can signal poisoning, attack, or data tampering, not just natural model decay.

column1: What is observability for AI systems?
column2: End-to-end telemetry (traces, metrics, logs) of prompts, retrievals, tool calls, and outputs for monitoring and debugging.

column1: What is cost/usage monitoring for AI?
column2: Tracking token and compute spend per user or app to detect abuse, anomalies, and unbounded consumption.
Input/output classifiers, anomaly detection, canary tokens, instruction-pattern matching, and monitoring for guardrail-bypass behavior.
Identifying inputs/outputs that attempt to bypass safety guardrails via classifiers, signatures, and behavioral monitoring.

column1: What is anomaly detection in AI ops?
column2: Flagging unusual prompts, outputs, query volumes, or tool calls that deviate from baselines to surface attacks.

column1: What are honeytokens/canaries in AI ops?
column2: Decoy secrets or data planted to trigger alerts when accessed or leaked, revealing compromise or exfiltration.

column1: What is SIEM integration for AI?
column2: Feeding AI logs and telemetry into a SIEM to correlate, alert, and run detections alongside other security data.
Detection rules aligned to MITRE ATLAS adversarial techniques to recognize known AI attack behaviors.

column1: What is detection engineering for AI?
column2: Building and tuning detections (rules, classifiers) for AI-specific attacks and integrating them into monitoring.
Tool-call logs, permission-use anomalies, and out-of-policy action attempts by the agent.

column1: What is threat hunting for AI systems?
column2: Proactively searching logs and telemetry for signs of injection, abuse, or compromise not caught by alerts.

column1: What changes in an AI-aware IR lifecycle?
column2: Standard IR phases plus AI-specific containment (disable model/tool, revoke keys), model rollback, and prompt or data-leak handling.
Disable the model/tool, revoke API keys and credentials, trigger a kill switch, and isolate affected pipelines.

column1: What is a model kill switch?
column2: A control to immediately disable a model/agent or its tool access to halt harmful behavior.

column1: Why revoke/rotate keys during AI incidents?
column2: To cut off attacker access via compromised credentials and stop abuse of model and tool endpoints.

column1: What is an AI incident playbook?
column2: A predefined procedure for responding to specific AI incidents (jailbreak, data leak, poisoning) with roles and steps.

column1: How to respond to a prompt-injection incident?
column2: Contain the affected flow, identify the injection source, patch inputs and guardrails, rotate exposed secrets, and review logs.

column1: How to respond to PII exposure via an LLM?
column2: Contain the output, assess scope, notify per regulation, rotate exposed secrets, and remediate the source (training data, RAG, or prompt).

column1: How to respond to system-prompt leakage?
column2: Rotate any exposed secrets, move sensitive logic and controls outside the prompt, patch the leak vector, and review for downstream abuse.

column1: How to respond to a poisoned-model discovery?
column2: Roll back to a trusted version, quarantine the artifact, trace provenance, retrain on clean data, and tighten supply-chain controls.
Reverting to a previous trusted model version after a compromise, poisoning, or faulty deployment.

column1: What backups matter for AI continuity?
column2: Versioned backups of models, vector indexes, prompts, and configs to enable restore and rollback.

column1: What is graceful degradation for AI?
column2: Designing systems to fall back to safe, limited functionality when the AI fails or is under attack, preserving core service.

column1: What is failover for AI services?
column2: Routing to redundant models, endpoints, or providers when the primary fails to maintain availability.

column1: What is business continuity for AI?
column2: Planning to keep critical AI-dependent services running during outages or attacks, including fallbacks and recovery objectives.

column1: Defense against cost bombing / denial of wallet?
column2: Rate limits, token and spend budgets with alerts, per-key quotas, anomaly detection, and auto-throttling.
Identifying, tracking, prioritizing, and remediating vulnerabilities in models, dependencies, and AI infrastructure.
To fix discovered vulnerabilities, jailbreak bypasses, and dependency CVEs, and to remove unsafe behaviors.
A process for researchers to responsibly report AI flaws (jailbreaks, leaks) so they can be fixed.
Gathering and applying intel on emerging AI attack techniques, malicious models, and jailbreaks to inform defenses.

column1: What is purple teaming for AI?
column2: Collaborative red and blue team exercises to test and improve AI detection and response together.

column1: What is a post-incident review for AI?
column2: Structured analysis after an AI incident to capture root cause, lessons, and improvements to controls and detections.

column1: What is a continuous-improvement feedback loop for AI security?
column2: Feeding incident, red-team, and eval findings back into controls, detections, and training to reduce recurrence.
